title: Bee stings: minimising and managing
subtitle: What reduces sting risk and what to do when it happens.
excerpt: Getting stung is part of beekeeping, but experienced beekeepers get stung far less than beginners. Most stings happen for predictable reasons: wrong weather, slow movements, wrong clothing, or disturbing the colony near the entrance. Understanding the patterns cuts the frequency; knowing what to do next handles the rest.
sourceNotes: BBKA guide to protective equipment and sting management (2022). NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit, nationalbeeunit.com. Anaphylaxis UK guidance on insect venom allergy, anaphylaxis.org.uk.

glossaryTerms.definition: A chemical signal (primarily isoamyl acetate, which smells of banana) released when a bee stings. It recruits guard bees and makes the colony temporarily more defensive. Moving away from the sting site reduces exposure.

content.text: Honeybees sting defensively, not aggressively. A bee on a flower does not sting; a bee defending her hive does. The behaviours that reduce sting risk are not complicated, and most beekeepers with a few seasons of experience get through the whole summer with fewer than a dozen stings.
Wrong weather: cold, overcast, or stormy days make colonies defensive. Inspect only on warm sunny days above 15°C with light wind.
Scent triggers: strong perfume, freshly cut grass, and dark colours near the head all increase sting risk. Avoid all three.
Blocking the entrance: standing directly in front of the hive entrance puts you across the flight path and triggers guard bees.
Crushed bees: rolling a bee at the frame-box junction releases
and recruit more defensive bees. Work slowly and deliberately.

New beekeepers wear a full suit and gloves for every inspection; there is no reason not to. Experienced beekeepers sometimes inspect without gloves in midsummer on gentle colonies, but the gain is only convenience and the risk is real. Any gaps at cuffs or ankles get found quickly. Tuck trousers into boots and check the veil zip before opening the hive.
Scrape the sting sideways with a fingernail or the flat edge of the hive tool immediately. Pinching it to pull it out squeezes more venom in. Move away from the hive, as the sting site releases
. A cold compress and antihistamine cream reduces the normal local reaction. Swelling is expected; a sting on the hand may swell to the wrist and elbow, this is a normal histamine response.

A systemic allergic reaction (throat tightening, widespread hives, difficulty breathing, dizziness within minutes of a sting) is a medical emergency. Call 999 immediately. This is distinct from the normal local swelling around a sting site, which resolves over a few days without treatment. Any beekeeper who has had a systemic reaction should discuss venom immunotherapy with their GP before continuing to keep bees.
